draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Users want personality and energy injected into functional running feedback
facade.label: Peppy voice with emojis
facade.acceptImplies: Design conversation tone, use emoji in notifications, celebrate splits and PRs with celebratory language
facade.rejectImplies: Stick with minimal, plain-text, no-nonsense interface — let data speak for itself

facade.hypothesis: Runners prioritize quick glance readability over visual warmth; a clean, high-contrast design reduces cognitive load during active use.
facade.label: Bright, minimal interface
facade.acceptImplies: Pursue crisp, data-forward aesthetics with stark backgrounds and bold typography; minimize texture and emotional flourish.
facade.rejectImplies: Explore warmer, more tactile design—softer gradients, rounded forms, and ambient visual breathing room that feels less clinical.

evidence.hypothesis: Users want personality and energy injected into functional running feedback
evidence.implication: Design conversation tone, use emoji in notifications, celebrate splits and PRs with celebratory language

facade.hypothesis: Runners care about moment-by-moment pace data (mile splits, lap times) more than trend visualization—they want atomic metrics they can tap to expand, not rolled-up charts.
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data (splits/segments) vs. Aggregated narrative (pace trend)
facade.label: Tap to see splits
facade.acceptImplies: Design around tappable metric cards showing individual split times; reserve charts for secondary detail view or post-run analysis.
facade.rejectImplies: Assume runners primarily want to see pace trends and rolling averages in the main view; hide granular split data behind settings.

facade.hypothesis: Weather apps for runners favor high-contrast, vibrant typography to surface critical info (temp, pace alerts) quickly during motion
facade.axisTargeted: Color intensity and readability under varied lighting
facade.label: Bold colored text
facade.acceptImplies: Explore saturated palettes, high-contrast text hierarchies, bright accent colors for alerts
facade.rejectImplies: Test softer, more muted color schemes with lower contrast and gentler typography weight

facade.hypothesis: Runners need quick access to multiple data layers (current conditions, hourly forecast, route-specific alerts) without deep navigation
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation structure: sidebar/persistent tabs vs modal/sequential disclosure
facade.label: Split view with tabs
facade.acceptImplies: User values parallel data access and reduced taps; probe next with density and card layout within this structure
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers sequential focus (one view at a time); next probe should test single-pane designs with strong scroll or pagination

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance info at top vs. scrollable detailed forecast layers
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather only
Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, instant updates and alerts

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance info at top vs. scrollable detailed forecast layers
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather only
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, instant updates and alerts
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Runners expect frictionless, momentum-based scrolling through weather data rather than snappy, page-by-page transitions—mimicking the flow state of running itself.
facade.axisTargeted: fluid continuous motion vs snappy discrete steps
facade.label: Smooth scroll to run
facade.acceptImplies: Prioritize inertial scrolling, easing curves, and dwell-based reveals for weather cards. Build momentum into micro-interactions.
facade.rejectImplies: Consider tab-based or paginated layouts with instant transitions. Weather data might need clarity over flow—snappier tap-response preferred.

facade.hypothesis: Runners respond better to friendly, chat-like language than formal or clinical tone, especially during active runs when cognitive load is high
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and personality — friendly vs professional
facade.label: Casual, conversational tone
facade.acceptImplies: Design copy as if talking to a running buddy; use contractions, direct address, and warm humor in alerts and feedback
facade.rejectImplies: Voice should remain neutral or professional; runners need clear, unadorned information without personality distractions

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.
